title: Dryopteris ferruginea
intro: Dryopteris ferruginea, also known as the rusty buckler fern, is a species of flowering plant in the family Dryopteridaceae, characterized by its dark green, lance-shaped fronds with rusty-brown scales. The plant is rich in health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which have been traditionally used to treat a range of conditions, including arthritis, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of D. ferruginea include flavonoids, sesquiterpenes, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, D. ferruginea is commonly prepared as a tea or tincture, and is also used in combination with other herbs to make topical creams and ointments for wound healing and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Dryopteris ferruginea is a species of fern within the genus Dryopteris, which is a group of ferns belonging to the family Dryopteridaceae. This family is part of the larger group known as Polypodiales, which includes various species of ferns with two-ranked fronds. The Polypodiales are a clade of ferns that have been recognized as a distinct group based on molecular and morphological characteristics. Dryopteris ferruginea's classification within this group reflects its evolutionary relationships with other fern species. Its classification is also used to distinguish it from other similar species within the same genus.
